data.title: PLATTS: 100--US FERC tees up action on PJM colocation proceeding, Southgate pipeline extension
data.description: (Platts)--12Dec2025/246 pm EST/1946 GMT **FERC to vote on PJM colocation proceeding **Also Mountain Valley Pipeline's Southgate project The US Federal Energy Regulatory Commission appears poised to act on a long-stalled proceeding that aimed to set guidelines for colocation agreements in the PJM Interconnection. FERC is also poised to act on Mountain Valley Pipeline's amended Southgate project at its December meeting. According to the sunshine notice ahead of the commission's Dec. 18 meeting, FERC appears likely to vote on the show-cause proceeding (EL25-49) that the commission initiated in February, which asked PJM to investigate colocation issues across the grid operator's 13-state footprint. PJM responded to the proceeding in March, giving the commission several suggestions — including allowing colocated loads to function as a demand response or emergency resource — but maintained its tariff was just and reasonable pertaining to colocation agreements. The PJM proceeding came amid complaints over regulatory uncertainty in the PJM region after FERC rejected the Susquehanna Nuclear plant's amended interconnection agreement with PPL to provide power to an Amazon data center campus. Susquehanna Nuclear is owned by Talen Energy. The PJM proceeding has stalled at the commission since PJM's response in March. Former Chairman Mark Christie said that he was unable to get two additional votes on a response to PJM before he departed the commission in August. However, with a new commissioner and a new chair, the dynamics of the commission have changed since August. Both Chairman Laura Swett and Commissioner David LaCerte emphasized powering infrastructure to support data center growth during their confirmation hearings in September. LARGE LOAD INTERCONNECTION The December vote comes amid a thorny debate among US energy stakeholders over the commission's jurisdiction over the interconnection of large loads and data centers. On Oct. 23, Energy Secretary Chris Wright sent an advance notice of proposed rulemaking (ANOPR) to FERC that asked the commission to assert jurisdiction over large load interconnection — a proposal that has been controversial among state entities. "We think the most likely outcome is that FERC could direct PJM to develop the additional 'conceptual' colocation approaches that the market operator offered earlier this year to address the commission's concerns that the treatment of colocation in the tariff has become unjust and unreasonable," ClearView Energy said in a Dec. 12 research note. "That said, we would not rule out the prospect that the commission either accepts PJM's response that its existing tariff already provides just and reasonable options for generators and large loads (e.g., data centers) to pursue co-location arrangements, or alternatively sets this matter for hearing while FERC staff work on a broader large load interconnection rulemaking in response to" Wright's ANOPR. While some stakeholders thought the DOE ANOPR would function as a replacement for the PJM proceeding, others — including the Electric Power Supplier Association — urged the commission to act on the PJM docket. "This rulemaking process should not interfere with advanced transactions or arrangements between large load customers and power suppliers or further delay proceedings like the PJM Show Cause," EPSA, which represents competitive power suppliers, said in comments filed to the commission in November. "In some circumstances, this may bode for the continued development (and approval) of regional programs, tariffs, and processes to facilitate new projects." The commission will also likely vote on a Sierra Club complaint (EL24-96) that alleged the Southwest Power Pool's accreditation rules for thermal and renewable resources were unjust and unreasonable. GAS ITEMS FERC will also vote on whether to authorize Mountain Valley Pipeline's amendment (CP25-205) to the Southgate natural gas pipeline. The 550,000 Dth/d project would extend 31 miles from the MVP terminus in Virginia to the new delivery points in Rockingham County, North Carolina, serving utilities in the Southeast. Staff of the commission recommended a finding of no significant impact for MVP's proposed amendment, which would reduce the extension to 31 miles from 75 miles and remove one planned compressor, but increase the capacity to 550,000 Dth/d from 375,000 Dth/d (CP25-60). The project has seen opposition from environmental groups who pointed to problems with sedimentation and erosion that occurred during construction of the mainline pipeline. As part of its review, FERC faced assertions from Williams that its neighboring 1.6 Bcf/d South System Expansion pipeline could supply the Southgate customers with fewer environmental impacts. The Southgate project's utility shippers have written to attest to the MVP extension's benefits, including diversifying gas resources and promoting competition among interstate pipelines. Also on the agenda is the Golden Pass LNG terminal's proposed natural gas supply lateral (CP25-205). If approved, the lateral would move up to 2.6 million Dth/d of gas to the terminal and would connect to a new gas pipeline being developed by Kinder Morgan's Trident Intrastate Pipeline and a new interstate header being developed by Kinder Morgan Louisiana Pipeline. The project cleared FERC's environmental review process in September. — George Weykamp, george.weykamp@spglobal.com; Maya Weber, maya.weber@spglobal.com-- Edited by Meghan Gordon, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--

data.title: Cannabis Stocks Surge. Trump May Reclassify Marijuana — but White House Says a Final Decision Hasn't Been Made. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni

A surge in cannabis stocks Tilray Brands and Canopy Growth following reports that President Donald Trump would loosen federal restrictions on marijuana may have been too enthusiastic.

The president indicated as far back as August that he was weighing the positives and negatives of a policy change. "I've heard great things having to do with medical, and I've heard bad things having to do with just about everything else," Trump said at the time, referring to the uses of the drug.

Media reports suggested Trump was planning to reclassify marijuana as a Schedule III drug, removing it from the Schedule I list. That would move marijuana out of a group that includes heroin and put it among substances such as combination pain medications.

While that wouldn't make marijuana legal for recreational use at the federal level, such a change would alter the tax requirements for cannabis companies.

Under a provision known as Section 280E of the Internal Revenue Code, companies dealing in Schedule I drugs are limited from deducting business expenses such as payroll and marketing costs when calculating their federal taxable income. Reclassification could do away with this burden.

The possibility of reclassification sent shares of cannabis companies through the roof. Tilray Brands and Canopy Growth, both of which also trade on the Toronto Stock Exchange, soared 35% and 46% in the U.S., respectively. Innovative Industrial Properties, a real estate investment trust for the cannabis industry, shot up 11%.

Exchange-traded funds also were soaring. The Amplify Seymour Cannabis ETF, which trades under the ticker CNBS, jumped 36%. The AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF ( MSOS), also gained 36%, heading for its largest same-day percentage increase on record.

It's too soon to tell whether the reports mean anything. A White House official told Barron's no final decision had been made regarding the rescheduling of the drug.

Plus, we've been here before. Four months ago, Trump told reporters he planned to decide in the coming weeks whether to change the way marijuana was regulated.

Cannabis stocks had the same reaction at the time. Tilray leapt 41% on Aug. 11 following Trump's remarks, while Canopy soared 26%.

In any case, enacting change at the federal level has been slow. The Justice Department proposed making marijuana a Schedule III substance last year, and the Drug Enforcement Administration still was moving through the review process when Trump took office in January.

Both stocks have fallen sharply over the past three years. Tilray's U.S.-listed stock has tumbled 15% in 2025 alone, while the S&P 500 index has risen by nearly the same amount. Canopy stock is down 40%.

History says it is unrealistic to expect a change soon. Friday's outsize gains are a sign some investors remain hopeful.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: IIOT-OXYS, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
IIOT-OXYS, Inc., a company specializing in designing, building, testing, and selling Edge Computing Systems for the Industrial Internet, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter of 2025. The report provides a detailed overview of the company's financial performance and operational challenges, reflecting its ongoing efforts to achieve profitability and maintain liquidity.

Financial Highlights

Revenues: The company reported no revenue for the three and nine months ended September 30, 2025, compared to $2,500 for the nine months ended September 30, 2024.

Gross Profit: With no revenue and cost of sales, the gross profit remained at zero for the reporting periods.

Net Loss: $(408,388) for the three months ended September 30, 2025, and $(590,928) for the nine months ended September 30, 2025. The company continued to incur net losses, reflecting ongoing challenges in achieving profitability.

Net Loss Attributable to Common Stockholders: $(438,889) for the three months ended September 30, 2025, and $(669,613) for the nine months ended September 30, 2025. The net loss attributable to common stockholders increased compared to the previous year.

Net Profit (Loss) Per Share Attributable to Common Stockholders - Basic and Diluted: $(0.00). The net loss per share remained unchanged, indicating no improvement in earnings per share.

Business Highlights

Business Focus: IIOT-OXYS, Inc. is primarily engaged in designing, building, testing, and selling Edge Computing Systems for the Industrial Internet. The company is focused on developing and marketing engineered products, software, and services for applications in the Industrial Internet, which involves collecting and processing data from various industrial systems and machines.

Operational Challenges: The company is facing significant operational challenges, including continuing operating losses, a working capital deficit, and substantial doubt about its ability to continue as a going concern. Management is actively seeking additional borrowings and/or sale of equity securities to maintain operations.

Segment Information: The company operates as a single reportable segment, focusing on the financial results of IIOT-OXYS, Inc. This indicates a streamlined operational focus without distinct divisions or product lines.

Future Outlook: Management believes that the company will be able to achieve a satisfactory level of liquidity to meet its obligations for the next twelve months through additional borrowings and/or sale of equity securities. However, there is no assurance that sufficient liquidity will be generated to maintain operations.

Revenue Recognition: The company recognizes revenue when products are delivered to the customer or services are performed, in accordance with ASC Topic No. 606, Revenue from Contracts with Customers. This includes identifying contracts, performance obligations, and recognizing revenue as obligations are satisfied.

Use of Estimates: The company regularly evaluates estimates and assumptions related to the valuation of accounts payable, accrued liabilities, and payable to related parties. These estimates are based on current facts, historical experience, and various other factors.

Recent Accounting Pronouncements: The company is evaluating the potential impact of the adoption of ASU 2023-09, which relates to improvements in income tax disclosures, effective for annual periods beginning after December 15, 2024.

SEC Filing: IIOT-OXYS, Inc. [ ITOX ] - 10-Q - Dec. 12, 2025

data.title: PLATTS: 100--MISO declares cold weather alert Dec. 13-25 as power prices, peakload climb
data.description: (Platts)--12Dec2025/243 pm EST/1943 GMT **Indiana Hub on-peak day-ahead offered at $115/MWh **MISO forecast peakload to surpass 99 GW on Dec. 15 The Midcontinent Independent System Operator peakload is expected to spike as temperatures dip, leading the grid operator to declare a cold weather alert for Dec. 13-25. The alert is in effect from 7 pm ET Dec. 13 through midnight Dec. 15 for MISO's south, central and north regions. "MISO and our member utilities are closely monitoring weather conditions as arctic air is forecast for our north, central and south regions," MISO spokesperson McKenzie Barbknecht told Platts. Cold weather alert declarations are a standard action for MISO when extreme weather is anticipated to drive up power demand, Barbknecht added. "They are designed to maximize MISO's ability to operate the grid reliably during weather events by providing situational awareness messaging and specific instructions to our members," Barbknecht said of cold-weather alerts. Operators are instructed to prepare for cold or extreme weather and implement plans to winterize units and plants to ensure availability during emergency conditions, according to the alert. In addition, operators are asked to review outage plans to determine if any maintenance or testing, scheduled or being performed on any monitoring, control, generation or transmission equipment can be deferred, revoked, or canceled. MARKET REACTION MISO forecast peakload to surpass 99 GW by Dec. 15, a nearly three-month high. Peakload has averaged 89.67 GW month to date, an increase of 5.3% from 2024. Driving up heating demand, population-weighted temperatures across the MISO footprint were forecast to fall as low as 7.4 degrees Fahrenheit on Dec. 14, according to CustomWeather data. So far in December, temperatures have averaged 23.14 F, down 30% year over year. Plunging temperatures and climbing demand drove up power prices in Dec. 12 trading. MISO's Indiana Hub on-peak day-ahead was offered at $115/MWh for Dec. 15 delivery on the Intercontinental Exchange, while the on-peak weekend was bid at $75/MWh and offered at $80/MWh. In comparison, the on-peak locational marginal price has averaged about $66.25/MWh so far in December, up 60% year over year, according to MISO data. WEATHER FORECAST The US National Weather Service forecast the coldest temperatures to be across the Northern Plains, Upper Mississippi Valley and Mid-West region, where arctic air will push southeast. Afternoon high temperatures will be 20 to 30 degrees below average Dec. 12-13 over the Northern Plains, spreading into the Upper Mississippi Valley on Dec. 13. A few record-low afternoon highs will be possible Dec. 13 over the Northern Plains/Upper Mississippi Valley region, with more widespread record-low afternoon temperatures possible Dec. 14 across the Ohio and Tennessee Valley regions. "A large area of moderate to heavy snows will spread from the Northern Plains, southeastward into the Middle Mississippi Valley on Friday, associated with the aforementioned arctic air outbreak," the weather service said in its Dec. 12 short-range forecast discussion. "This snow, driven by a mid-level disturbance, will continue east into the Mid-West and Central Appalachians on Dec. 13." — Kassia Micek, kassia.micek@spglobal.com-- Edited by Meghan Gordon, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--

data.title: Trump Is Sued Over White House Ballroom Construction — WSJ
data.description:
By Victoria Albert

A historical-preservation group sued Friday over President Trump's effort to demolish the White House's East Wing and build an expansive ballroom in its place, urging a court to pause construction pending further review.

The lawsuit, filed by the National Trust for Historic Preservation in the United States, alleges that the destruction of the East Wing and the planned construction of the ballroom should have been subject to several federal reviews before it ever began. The project is set to add at least 90,000 square feet to the White House complex.

The suit was filed in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia. It accuses Trump and administration officials of violating laws that required outside input on the project's environmental, historic and aesthetic impacts, and said construction must stop until such reviews are complete.

"No president is legally allowed to tear down portions of the White House without any review whatsoever — not President Trump, not President Biden, and not anyone else," the trust, a private nonprofit chartered by Congress to support preservation efforts, said in the suit.

A White House spokesman said the renovation efforts were within the president's purview. "President Trump has full legal authority to modernize, renovate, and beautify the White House — just like all of his predecessors did," the spokesman said.

Write to Victoria Albert at victoria.albert@wsj.com

data.title: PLATTS: 100--Market dynamics, capacity crunch to keep PJM auction prices at the cap
data.description: (Platts)--09Dec2025/323 pm EST/2023 GMT **Interconnection logjam slows new capacity **Utilities mull two-tier auction for existing, new capacity Market dynamics and a continued imbalance of generation supply to meet strong electricity demand will once again push PJM Interconnection capacity prices to a federally approved cap, experts said. A price cap of $333.44/MW-day and floor of $179.55/MW-day have been established for the 2027-28 base residual auction, according to PJM. Results for the 2026-27 base residual auction are scheduled to be posted Dec. 17. The bidding window opened Dec. 4 and closes Dec. 10. The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission in April approved an agreement between PJM and Pennsylvania Governor Josh Shapiro that sets a price cap of $325/MW-day and a price floor of $175/MW-day for the 2026-27 and 2027-28 auctions. PJM's capacity auction for the 2026-27 delivery year officially cleared at a record high of $329.17/MW-day based on a combination of factors, including tighter supply and higher demand. This comes after the auction for PJM's 2025-26 delivery year cleared at $269.92/MW-day for much of the grid operator's 13-state footprint, a nearly tenfold increase from the $28.92/MW-day clearing price in the 2024-25 auction. Auction prices are expected to remain "at the cap ... because of the difficulty of getting the capacity online in time to satisfy the reliability requirement," Tanya Peevey, a senior principal analyst with S&P Global Energy CERA, said in a Dec. 1 interview. Peevey, however, said she was "less hopeful a year ago than now" that more capacity would stay online to help drive down prices. "The efforts to keep gas plants online and to bring new [capacity] into the fray could help to alleviate some of those upward price pressures," Peevey told Platts, part of S&P Global Energy. "I think for [2027-28], the higher likelihood is to hit the cap," Peevey said. INTERCONNECTION LOGJAM PJM is also dealing with a logjam of intermittent generation as the nation's largest grid operator works to meet surging demand largely tied to data centers. In response, FERC approved PJM's proposal in February to enable 50 "shovel-ready, high-reliability" generation projects to move ahead in the interconnection queue as part of its Reliability Resource Initiative. "To our understanding, a lot of projects are moving through the queue, but we're still not seeing, as of right now, the uplift that would tend to take us maybe down below that cap," said Steve Piper, director of North America power and renewables research with S&P Global Energy CERA. There is also uncertainty around how much demand response resources will help drive down prices. "Ahead of the last auction, we certainly heard from some buy-side folks who had a lot of optimism about demand response contributing, and kind of the thesis was that these prices should be a really good signal for demand response providers," Piper said. "And so, what ultimately cleared was, I think, 'disappointing' was the term used." In addition, federal policy uncertainty persists related to a notice of proposed rulemaking for large loads. An Oct. 23 advance notice of proposed rulemaking from the Department of Energy asks FERC to assert jurisdiction over the interconnection of large loads. "If data centers themselves represent a form of demand response or quasi-demand response, what does that mean for the conventional providers of demand response?" Piper asked. "Does that end up sort of taking up their market niche or something like that? It's a little bit of a sideline, but something to keep an eye on." TWO-AUCTION SYSTEM? FirstEnergy Chair, President and CEO Brian Tierney is among executives at investor-owned utilities that have been critical of the current construct of the PJM capacity auction, especially as it relates to resource adequacy and bill affordability. "The thing that is really driving a lot of the [cost] increases is you're having an increase in load, consuming generating capacity faster than what we thought it would be consumed at, and it's driving the price for capacity up higher and higher because we have these capacity auctions that don't really work the way they're supposed to," Tierney told Platts in a recent interview. Akron, Ohio-headquartered FirstEnergy, which operates transmission and distribution utilities in five states in the PJM region, has expressed interest in investing in generation resources under the appropriate construct. But Tierney said PJM should consider implementing a "two-tiered structure." "One that would pay existing capacity one price and another structure that would attract incremental capacity," Tierney said on the company's third-quarter earnings call in October. FirstEnergy would likely bid its new capacity in West Virginia into such a construct, the CEO said. "We would bid our new capacity into the new capacity auction," Tierney said. "And any dollars that we would get for the portion above and beyond what our West Virginia customers were consuming would be used to reduce our West Virginia customers' bills while still providing that capacity to the regional market." Monitoring Analytics, PJM's independent market monitor, recommends that PJM create a queue for data centers that would not be connected to the grid until there is adequate capacity, and a fast-track queue process for data centers that bring their own generation. Data centers represented more than $9 billion of the $12 billion increase in the July 2024 base residual auction, according to Monitoring Analytics. — Darren Sweeney, Darren.Sweeney@spglobal.com-- Edited by Gary Gentile, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--

data.title: Oracle-Broadcom one-two punch hits AI trade, but investor optimism persists
data.description:
Key points:
  • Oracle's AI spending raises valuation and debt concerns
  • Investors become selective in AI stock investments
  • Short sellers show little appetite to ramp up bearish bets against AI stocks
  • Investors encouraged by broad market strength amid AI trade weakness

By Saqib Iqbal Ahmed and Lewis Krauskopf

The red-hot trade backing artificial intelligence-related stocks has taken a bruising from back-to-back troubling updates from Oracle and Broadcom, reigniting concerns about frothy valuations and an AI bubble.

Still, investors say reasons for optimism about AI remain intact, and many are wary about calling a top.

Investors have piled into AI-related companies this year as the technology has taken off, with promises to make Corporate America more efficient.

But some investors feel AI-related shares are overvalued. High-profile names such as Michael Burry have been bearish, comparing the recent AI boom to the 1990s dot-com era. For now, however, short-selling has been limited to smaller companies, with few pressing bearish bets against the biggest AI names.

The latest concerns centered on Oracle and Broadcom. Oracle's shares slumped as much as 17% since Wednesday's close after the company, which has taken on debt to finance its ambitious AI spending, warned capital expenditures for fiscal 2026 are now expected to be $15 billion higher than it estimated in September.

Oracle stock came under fresh pressure on Friday following a Bloomberg report that the company has pushed back the completion dates for some of the data centers it is developing for OpenAI to 2028 from 2027.

Broadcom shares fell more than 11% on Friday, a day after the chipmaker warned growing sales of lower-margin custom AI processors were squeezing profitability, sparking worries that the business may be less lucrative.

The hit to Oracle and Broadcom weighed on other tech shares during the day, as investors worried about AI spending and the lack of a clear timeline on returns from the investments. The tech-heavy Nasdaq sank 1.4%, while the S&P 500 Index fell 0.9% on Friday afternoon, a day after finishing at a record high.

"I still think the AI trade is intact," said Chuck Carlson, chief executive at Horizon Investment Services in Hammond, Indiana. "I don't think this is the beginning of a sustained significant decline."

The turbulence this week for AI-linked stocks seemed similar to shorter-term selling in the wake of the DeepSeek news earlier this year and other AI "blips downward" in 2025, said Carlson.

He was not recommending that clients sell AI-exposed stocks such as Nvidia , and said the outlook for many such stocks remained solid.

"Is there going to be potentially more subdued performance in those stocks (heading into 2026)? Yeah, but I mean, they've been gangbusters and it's hard to maintain that," he added.

Oracle, on Thursday, declined to comment on its share price move or the AI trade. Broadcom did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Friday.

MORE SCRUTINY

Investors have been growing more picky in the AI space, some market participants said, with less willingness to indiscriminately reward spending on AI.

"You've seen this really positive correlation between really aggressive capital spending and stock prices ... That has changed pretty significantly beneath the surface over the last couple of months," said Mark Hackett, chief market strategist at Nationwide.

In late November, Meta shares slumped 11% after the Facebook and Instagram parent forecast "notably larger" capital expenses next year due to AI investments, including aggressively building data centers.

Capital spending, a crucial component of the AI trade, has propelled equities following the launch of AI assistant ChatGPT in November 2022.

"I think a leadership shift is absolutely warranted and it's underway. But that doesn't mean the AI story switches off like a light switch," Hackett said.

SHORT STORY

Even investors who question the AI trade are wary of betting against it.

"I believe today the stock market is in a phase that could become a blow-off top of extreme magnitude on the upside,” Burry, the famed investor whose successful 2008 bets against the U.S. housing market were dramatized in the movie "The Big Short," wrote in a post.

Burry recently stepped up criticism of tech heavyweights including Nvidia and Palantir Technologies , questioning the cloud infrastructure boom. He has a short position on Palantir.

Two leading U.S. fund managers, who requested anonymity, said worries about a bubble are exaggerated. They said Big Tech "hyperscalers" were still struggling to meet unrelenting demand for more data centers.

"Across our basket of 61 AI-related stocks, we don’t yet see positioning that looks like investors aggressively betting on an AI bubble bursting," said Peter Hillerberg, cofounder at data and analytics company Ortex Technologies.

Investors have shown a pick-up in appetite to short the smaller and mid-cap AI names, yet the largest AI beneficiaries remain only lightly shorted, Ortex data showed as of Thursday.

"We have seen stock-specific spikes in short interest around earnings and headline risk in certain AI-linked names, including Oracle, and some of those trades will naturally look smarter after sharp post-results moves," Hillerberg said.

"But taken together, the data look more like targeted skepticism in individual AI stories than a broad, coordinated attempt to call the top of an AI bubble."

data.title: Jamie Dimon Weighs In on Fed Chair Candidates at Closed-Door Event — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Matt Peterson and Rebecca Ungarino

JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon, the nation's top banker, has some thoughts on the next Federal Reserve chair.

At his bank's private, annual asset-management industry event held this week, Dimon was asked about Kevin Hassett, a top White House economic adviser who is seen as the likeliest successor to Fed Chair Jerome Powell, and Kevin Warsh, a former Fed governor who President Donald Trump considered for chair during his first term.

Hassett, Dimon said, would be most likely to lower interest rates in the short-term, in line with what Trump has said he wants from the Fed.

"Kevin Warsh would make a great chairman," Dimon said, according to people familiar with his remarks.

Dimon said at the event that his firm would be fine under either man.

Trump had described Warsh as one of his top three candidates during the summer but has lately been hinting that Hassett is his choice.

A spokesman for the White House didn't immediately return a request for comment.

Trump has been eager to replace Powell, whom the president blames for keeping interest rates too high. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is leading a search for the new chair. He has developed a shortlist of five candidates, including both Hassett and Warsh.

As the longtime chief of the largest U.S. lender, Dimon has cultivated a reputation as a de facto spokesman for the banking industry's interests.

He is well-known for speaking candidly in ways that CEOs with less gravitas wouldn't. Dimon has previously spoken out about the Fed's independence as Trump seeks a replacement for Powell.

"I think the independence of the Fed is absolutely critical," Dimon told reporters in July. "Playing around with the Fed can have adverse consequences, the absolute opposite of what you might be hoping for."

Write to Matt Peterson at matt.peterson@dowjones.com and Rebecca Ungarino at rebecca.ungarino@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Trump administration unlikely to finalize 2026 biofuel quotas this year, sources say
data.description:
Key points:
  • EPA still reviewing comments; no end-of-year decision expected
  • Delay folds quotas into broader oil-agriculture policy talks
  • Uncertainty complicates contracts, hedging, and investment decisions

By Jarrett Renshaw

The Trump administration is not expected to finalize 2026 biofuel-blending quotas before the end of this year, according to three sources familiar with the situation, extending uncertainty over a policy closely watched by the rival oil and agricultural sectors.

The slowdown would throw one of the administration's most consequential energy policy choices into next year and folds the highly anticipated quotas into a growing cluster of interlocking decisions the White House is weighing on biofuels policy.

Together, the moves have raised expectations the administration may look to strike a broader - albeit elusive - agreement between rival oil and agricultural interests.

The Environmental Protection Agency, which administers biofuels policy, has scheduled meetings with stakeholders on the quota rule early next, two sources told Reuters, a sign that the issue will be pushed into next year.

A third source also said the EPA's decision was unlikely by the end of this year.

The sources spoke about the matter on condition of anonymity.

The EPA told Reuters it was still reviewing public comments on the volume requirements and declined to offer any guidance on timing. The volume mandates are administered under the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS), the federal program that mandates the blending of biofuels such as ethanol and biodiesel into the U.S. fuel supply.

A delay in finalizing the mandates is important because fuel makers, farmers and commodity traders use them to lock in supply contracts, hedge volatile crop and energy markets, and justify investments in new production capacity.

Without clarity on next year's quotas, companies say they are forced to hold back on deals and delay spending decisions that shape biofuel output and margins.

WHITE HOUSE LOOKS FOR COMMON GROUND

Earlier this year, the EPA proposed raising total renewable fuel requirements for 2026 and 2027, significantly increasing targets for advanced biofuels and biomass-based diesel targets while tightening rules on the use of imported biofuels.

The EPA had initially been expected to finalize the proposal by the end of October.

The quotas have increasingly been swept into a broader set of critical energy and agricultural policy decisions the administration is weighing in the coming weeks - issues that appear separate on their own but could be linked as part of a wider bargain between rival oil and agricultural interests, the sources said.

Those issues include legislative efforts to allow year-round sales of gasoline containing higher blends of ethanol, a proposal to penalize imported biofuels and feedstocks, and unresolved questions over whether and how larger refiners could be required to make up for gallons exempted from the RFS.

The White House has held several meetings in recent weeks with oil and agricultural groups in hopes of finding common ground.

Asked whether these issues are becoming increasingly connected, the EPA said it is "committed to strengthening American energy security and supporting American farmers."

data.title: PLATTS: 100--Consumers Energy, Michigan AG battle over rate rules for data centers
data.description: (Platts)--12Dec2025/238 pm EST/1938 GMT **AG seeks rehearing over rules for large load customers **Dispute centers on existing ratepayers' cost exposure Michigan's attorney general and one of the state's biggest power utilities are battling before regulators over how to set rates for large load users in a way that ensures ordinary customers will not pay the costs of connecting data centers to the grid. Consumers Energy, a subsidiary of CMS Energy Corp., has condemned efforts by Michigan Attorney General Dana Nessel (D) to get the Michigan Public Service Commission (MPSC) to reverse a Nov. 6 order approving the utility's application to modify its tariff provisions for large load customers. "The attorney general's challenge has no merit," Kelly Hall, Consumers' senior vice president of regulatory and legal affairs, said in a Dec. 11 statement. "The MPSC's order last month creates a useful framework as Michigan grows and attracts new jobs and tax revenue for local communities." Nessel petitioned the MPSC on Dec. 8 to hold new hearings related to the order, which she said did not go far enough to protect existing ratepayers from having to pay for Consumers' costs to serve the new large load customers. Consumers has blasted Nessel's petition, saying the approved tariff amendments do protect existing residential and business ratepayers and warning that Nessel's challenge could discourage investment in the state. The MPSC order last month approved new terms and conditions for Consumers' primary demand rate for customers with a minimum service threshold of 100 MW, either from one site or from multiple locations 20 GW or larger owned by the same entity. The new terms require a 15-year minimum contract length as well as automatic, five-year contract extensions and a four-year notice period for contract termination. In addition, customers would have to pay for at least 80% of expected demand regardless of their actual use and would have to foot the bill for planning studies needed to develop a project. Customers would also be subject to an exit fee should they terminate service before a contract's end. In her petition asking the MPSC for a rehearing, Nessel said the commission "erred and acted arbitrarily and capriciously" in ignoring her previous request to establish a 20-year minimum contract term and require large load customers to pay for a minimum 90% of expected demand. AG SEEKS CONTESTED PROCEEDING Nessel also said the MPSC order would let Consumers Energy seek approval of new large load customers in ex parte fashion rather than via a contested proceeding, which offers greater opportunity for stakeholder input. "This finding will have the unintended consequence of undermining the ability for a full and robust vetting of cost-coverage for these customers, as well as adding a layer of unnecessary litigation and delay to that review," the attorney general's petition said. In addition, Nessel said the MPSC should have set a single-site threshold of 50 MW rather than 100 MW for the tariff. And she said the order should have required that large customers both cover the costs needed to comply with state renewable energy standards and procure at least 60% of their incremental energy needs from renewable sources. Danny Wimmer, Nessel's press secretary, defended the petition for a rehearing and said it was aimed at protecting state residents from "dishonest and predatory rate hike requests" from utilities. "Consumers Energy falsely contends the attorney general's position is anti-investment," Wimmer said In a Dec. 11 statement. "It is not, though Attorney General Nessel will continue to use the powers of her office to ensure the utility monopolies in this state act responsibly to their existing residential customers and not allow them to be harmed by the companies' pursuits of new data center customers." Nessel has sought to block efforts by another large Michigan utility, DTE Energy, to obtain MPSC approval via an ex parte proceeding for special contracts to provide service to a planned, 1.4-GW data center in Saline Township. After DTE submitted that request to the MPSC on Oct. 31, the attorney general intervened to ask the commission to treat the matter as a contested case, which would require full public hearings and allow ratepayers and other interested parties to participate. The MPSC has not yet ruled on DTE's request. — Jason Fargo, jason.fargo@spglobal.com-- Edited by Lyle Crouse, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--
